article_meta.title: Tillis: 'Noem Is Out of Her Depth,' She's 'Not Competent' 7 Pam Key28 Jan 2026 Wednesday on CNN's "The Lead," Sen

reasoning: The model commits severe attribution rule violations. All three flagged items are from Sen. Tillis's quoted speech, not the author's own prose. The headline uses Tillis's quoted words (in quotation marks), and the article body is almost entirely a transcription of Tillis's statements. The author's own prose is minimal and neutral ('said Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem "is not competent" to run the federal agency' — standard attribution). The model also uses bias type labels not in the defined list ('Negative Labeling', 'Partisan Framing', 'Loaded Language'). The correct output would likely be [] or flag only minor author-level structural choices.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: All three flagged items are from Tillis's quoted speech on CNN. The headline itself contains Tillis's words in quotation marks. The second and third flagged items are explicitly within Tillis's extended quoted statements. Zero items come from the author's own prose.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: All three flagged items are false positives because they are attributed source language, not author prose. None represent genuine author-level bias.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The article's author prose is very minimal — mostly neutral attribution ('said', 'He added'). There could be a case for Bias by Story Choice and Placement (Breitbart choosing to highlight intra-Republican criticism) or Slant (article is essentially a platform for one senator's criticism), but these are debatable. The model missed potential structural bias while flagging quoted text instead.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: All three labels — 'Negative Labeling', 'Partisan Framing', 'Loaded Language' — are not in the defined bias type list. The valid list includes specific terms like 'Word Choice', 'Slant', 'Spin', etc. The model invented category names.
